completenessNote: Curated summary of the checklist's most reusable offer-building actions. Because the source is already a checklist, the summary preserves stepwise action items.

items.title: Choose a market before choosing an offer
problemsSolved: Helps businesses avoid polishing an offer for a market with weak demand or poor economics.
items.text: The checklist starts with market and niche selection: pain, purchasing power, easy targeting, and growth. This makes market choice the first offer-design decision.
usageExample: A founder can rule out a fun niche with low purchasing power and instead choose a smaller but more monetizable segment that is actively looking for help.

items.title: Use premium pricing as part of value creation
problemsSolved: Helps operators understand that higher price can support both perception and results when the offer is real.
items.text: The checklist explicitly pushes the user to charge more than competitors and tie the price to value. The underlying logic is that higher price can increase buy-in, perceived seriousness, and the resources available to deliver results.
usageExample: A coach can stop copying the cheapest market rate and instead raise price after strengthening proof, support, and promise clarity.
sourceNotes: Sections: Charge What It’s Worth; Maximize Value Using Value Equation

items.title: Write every problem before you design the package
problemsSolved: Helps the offer solve the real job instead of only the founder's favorite part of the problem.
items.text: The checklist asks the builder to map dream outcome, every obstacle, every step, and what happens before and after the goal. This pushes deeper buyer understanding before packaging.
usageExample: An agency selling lead generation can list setup issues, ad fatigue, attribution confusion, follow-up bottlenecks, and what the buyer needs after leads arrive before deciding the deliverables.
sourceNotes: Sections: Create Your Core Offer Part 1 & 2

items.title: Use the delivery cube to create higher-value versions
problemsSolved: Helps businesses systematically vary effort, support, speed, medium, and format to increase value.
items.text: The delivery cube turns offer creation into a menu of delivery decisions rather than a single default service model. It helps generate options that can be sold at different value or cost levels.
usageExample: A community product can test one-to-many recorded delivery for scale, then add higher-ticket done-with-you support via Zoom and chat for premium buyers.

items.title: Trim low-value pieces and stack the strongest components
problemsSolved: Helps businesses avoid bloated offers that are expensive to fulfill but not compelling to buy.
items.text: The checklist's trim-and-stack section recommends keeping only high-value elements, limiting high-cost pieces, and letting the offer feel substantial because of the right stack, not sheer volume.
usageExample: A service firm can remove seven low-impact deliverables and instead package three strong value drivers plus one premium support element that actually closes sales.

items.title: Add scarcity and urgency ethically
problemsSolved: Helps buyers act sooner without weakening price through discounting.
items.text: The checklist pushes fixed caps, cohort timing, expiring bonuses, and time-sensitive opportunities to make the buyer decide. The emphasis is on bounded access rather than price-cutting.
usageExample: A consultant could limit intake to five clients per month and attach a start-date deadline rather than discounting to force action.

items.title: Use bonuses to solve objections instead of discounting
problemsSolved: Helps businesses increase yes-rates while preserving price and perceived value.
items.text: The checklist recommends stacking bonuses after price, giving them benefit-heavy names, assigning real value, and making them solve likely buyer objections. The bonus should make the core purchase easier to justify, not cheaper.
usageExample: A software company could add migration support, KPI templates, and an implementation checklist as bonuses instead of cutting the subscription price.
